coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and selective sensing system of metallothioneins based on carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Lian
Du, Xiaoyu
Zhao, Huijun
Sun, Yue
Liu, Wen
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we developed a sensitive and selective sensing system for the detection of metallothioneins (MTs). The system is based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). In this method, the fluorescence emission of CQDs was quenched by AuNPs due to FRET. When MTs were added to the CQD–AuNP system, the strong combination between thiol group and Au made the CQDs release from AuNPs, and the fluorescence of CQDs was recovered. The CQD–AuNP system can detect the MTs in aqueous solution (pH 3.0, citrate–HCl buffer) selectively and sensitively with a short response time (15 min). Results show that the fluorescence recovery efficiency has a good linear relationship with the MTs concentration in the range of 12–210 nmol L−1, and the limit of detection was 5.25 × 10−9 mol L−1. Furthermore, the sensing system was utilized to determine MTs in human urine samples with satisfactory results. The proposed system exhibits the advantages of high sensitivity, high selectivity, easy operation and most importantly, low cost and non-cytotoxicity to detect protein MTs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Metallothioneins

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CQDs; (B) Particle size distribution histogram of CQDs; (C) XRD pattern of CQDs; (D) FTIR spectrum of CQDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of CQDs. (B)-(D): High resolution C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of the CQDs.

(A) Emission spectra of the CQDs at excitation wavelength of 350–420 nm. (B) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs. The insets show the color of CQDs under visible light (left) and UV lamp of 365 nm (right).
Absorption spectrum of AuNPs (curve a) and fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs (curve b).

(A) Effects of different AuNPs concentrations on the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs (the inset shows the colors of CQDs added without and with AuNPs under UV illumination of 365 nm). (B) The relationship between F
0 /F and cAuNPs at 522 nm. The AuNP concentrations (from top to bottom): 0, 0.174, 0.348, 0.522, 0.696, 0.87, 1.044, 1.218, 1.392, 1.566, 1.74, 1.914, and 2.088 nM; CQDs: 0.02 mg mL−1.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) CQD–MT, (c) CQD–AuNP, (d) CQD–AuNP–MT, (e) MTs. (B) The absorption spectra of (a), CQDs, (b) AuNPs, (c) MTs, (d) CQD–AuNPs, (e) AuNP–MT, (f) CQD–AuNP–MT. CQDs:0.02 mg mL−1, AuNPs: 2.09 nM, MTs: 2.1 × 10−7 mol L−1.
(A) TEM images of (A) AuNPs; (B, C) CQD–AuNP at the absence of MTs; (D) CQD–AuNP in the presence of MTs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQD–AuNP in the presence of different concentrations of MTs (λex = 390 nm). (B) The calibration curve of (F–F0)/F0 versus the concentration of MTs. The concentrations of MTs (from bottom to top) were: 0, 1.2, 2.3, 4.7, 7.0, 9.4, 12, 14, 16, 19, 21 × 10−8 mol L−1. CQDs: 0.02 mg mL−1; AuNPs: 2.09 nM.

Interference of coexisting components on the fluorescence intensity of CQD–AuNP with MTs. CQDs: 0.02 mg mL−1; AuNPs: 2.09 nM; MTs: 2.1 × 10−7 mol L−1; the concentration of l-cysteine, glutathione: 1.0 × 10−6 mol L−1; the concentration of Pb2+, Mg2+, NO3-, Cl−, Cu2+, Fe2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Zn2+, SO42-, Cd2+, Histidine, Glycine, Glucose: 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1.
Schematic illustration of MTs detection based on FRET principle between CQDs and AuNPs.
Comparison of methods for the determination of MTs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: HPLC-AAS: High-performance liquid chromatography-atomic absorption spectrometry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results for human urine samples (n = 4).

head.ce:title: Sensitive and selective sensing system of metallothioneins based on carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Medical Science, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan, 030001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Basic Medical Science, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan, 030001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we developed a sensitive and selective sensing system for the detection of metallothioneins (MTs). The system is based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). In this method, the fluorescence emission of CQDs was quenched by AuNPs due to FRET. When MTs were added to the CQD–AuNP system, the strong combination between thiol group and Au made the CQDs release from AuNPs, and the fluorescence of CQDs was recovered. The CQD–AuNP system can detect the MTs in aqueous solution (pH 3.0, citrate–HCl buffer) selectively and sensitively with a short response time (15 min). Results show that the fluorescence recovery efficiency has a good linear relationship with the MTs concentration in the range of 12–210 nmol L−1, and the limit of detection was 5.25 × 10−9 mol L−1. Furthermore, the sensing system was utilized to determine MTs in human urine samples with satisfactory results. The proposed system exhibits the advantages of high sensitivity, high selectivity, easy operation and most importantly, low cost and non-cytotoxicity to detect protein MTs.

A method based on the principle of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) was developed for the detection of metallothioneins.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Metallothioneins (MTs) are a kind of low-molecular-weight (Mr < 10 kDa), cysteine-rich, metal-binding proteins that are widely found in various animals, higher plants, microorganisms, and various tissues and organs of humans [1,2]. As a class of heavy metal-binding proteins, MTs can be stably coordinated in the form of metal–thiolate clusters by thiol groups in cysteine residues and d10 metal ions, such as Zn2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+, to form a nontoxic or low-toxic complex [3]. MTs play an important role in the detoxification of heavy metals, scavenging of free radicals, regulation of intracellular balance, and maintenance of the homeostasis of metal ion concentrations. In addition, MTs are associated with several physiological processes, including the growth and development of the body, delaying aging, and certain diseases.

Various organisms have been reported to induce the expression of MTs in the body through exposure to heavy metals [4]. MTs are considered as potential biomarkers for heavy metal pollution in the environment because of their ability to respond to heavy metal contamination and the statistical correlation between MTs content in aquatic animals and heavy metal content in the environment [5]. Several studies have found a statistically significant relationship between the MTs content in human urine and the degree of environmental pollution caused by heavy metals [4,6,7]. As biomarkers for heavy metal pollution, MTs have attracted considerable attention in the fields of environmental science and bioscience toxicology. Hence, the establishment of a rapid, sensitive, convenient, and nontoxic method for the direct detection of MTs has important practical significance for the development of preventive management measures and timely monitoring of environmental pollution.

At present, there have been some reports on the detection of MTs, such as metal saturation determination [8], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [9], immunization [10], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11]. However, most of these methods have their own limitations, such as the lack of sufficient selectivity and sensitivity, the need for expensive complex equipment, or the involvement of cumbersome procedures. Although electrochemical techniques [12,13] have high sensitivity and can be quantified at very low concentration levels, the potential toxic vapor of mercury likely limits its wide application.

In recent years, fluorescence spectroscopy techniques have shown unique advantages in analytical and bioanalytical studies due to their operational simplicity, high sensitivity, and stability [14,15]. Although fluorescence spectroscopy can perform sensitive analysis on biological macromolecules, it cannot easily detect proteins that do not have native fluorescence, such as MTs. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) technology is a nonradiative phenomenon, in which the energy is transferred from the excited donor fluorophore to the ground-state acceptor through dipole–dipole coupling process [16,17]. FRET has been used for the detection of many biological molecules [18–20] and ions [21,22]. In addition, it has high detection sensitivity and precision and low cost. An excellent donor–acceptor pair is a significant factor in improving the efficiency of FRET. Carbon QDs (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with a nearly spherical shape and sizes below 10 nm [23]. Compared with semiconductor QDs or organic fluorescent dyes, CQDs have apparent advantages in terms of aqueous solubility, chemical stability, photostability, low cytotoxicity, and favorable biocompatibility [24,25]. Therefore, CQDs are widely used in sensing compounds due to its wide range of excitation spectra and adjustable emission spectrum [26,27]. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) can be considered as an efficient acceptor of FRET due to their high extinction coefficient and intense electronic absorption bands in the visible region [28].

In the present study, we used nitrogen-doped CQDs as fluorescent probes and AuNPs as a fluorescent quencher to construct a FRET fluorescence sensor to detect MTs. The AuNPs modified with citrate were negatively charged and could adsorb positively charged CQDs onto the surface of AuNPs to form CQD–AuNP nanocomposites via electrostatic interaction. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs was caused by the FRET between CQDs and AuNPs. However, when MTs were added, the adsorbed CQDs were released from the surface of AuNPs due to the specific binding of MTs to AuNPs. As a result, the CQDs fluorescence was restored. On the basis of this principle, a novel and simple “turn-on” fluorescence sensor can be constructed for the detection of MTs.

MT-I standard from rabbit liver was obtained from Beijing Yong Kang Jiaxin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The concentration of the MT-I stock solution was calculated as 1.17 × 10−5 mol L−1. The citrate-stabilized AuNPs (13 ± 1 nm) were purchased from Henan Najing Technology Co., Ltd. (Henan, China), and the concentration of its solution was 6.96 nM. Sodium citrate–hydrochloric acid buffer solution was used to adjust the pH of the solution in the experiment. l-Glutamic acid (Glu) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and 4-aminosalicylic acid (PAS) was purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). All other experiments were performed with analytical reagent grade chemicals, and deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 KV to further determine the sample morphology and structure. The DX-2700 X-ray diffractometer (Cu Kα rays, λ = 1.5418 Å, Shanghai, China) was used to measure the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CQD powder in the range of 5°–80° at a speed of 8°/min. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) from 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1 was performed on a BRUKER TENSOR 27 infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with Al Kα X-ray source for excitation. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were acquired on a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a scanning range of 200–700 nm. Fluorescence spectra were measured with a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Varian, USA) with the exciting and emission slits set at 5 and 10 nm, respectively. Fluorescent photographs of CQDs irradiated under UV light of 365 nm were analyzed using an ZF7 ultraviolet analyzer (Henan, China). The zeta potentials were recorded on a Nano ZS 90 zeta potential and particle size analyzer (Malvern, Britain). The pH of the buffer solution was adjusted using a FE20 METTLER TOLEDO pH meter (Shanghai, China).

Synthesis of CQDs by typical hydrothermal treatment: 0.5 g of l-glutamic acid and 1.04 g of 4-aminosalicylic acid were added to 20 mL of deionized water and stirred to a mixture. The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 7 h in an oven. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was removed from the oven and cooled to room temperature naturally. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the precipitation and then filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter. The clarified orange solution was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1 kDa) against deionized water for 24 h, and the CQD solution after dialysis was lyophilized to obtain nitrogen-doped CQD solid powder for further characterization and use. The solid was then dispersed in deionized water as a stock solution (1 mg mL−1).

CQDs (10 μL, 1 mg mL−1) and AuNPs (150 μL, 6.96 nM) were added into a 2 mL Eppendorf microcentrifuge tube, and the mixture was kept at room temperature for 5 min. Then, 30 μL citrate-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 3.0) and different amounts of MTs solution (1.17 × 10−6 mol L−1) were added to the prepared mixture, and each sample solution was diluted to a final volume of 500 μL with deionized water. After incubation at 50 °C for 15 min, the fluorescence spectra of the control (F0) and the sample group (F) were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer. F and F0 indicate the fluorescence intensities of the system with and without MTs, respectively.

Urine samples were collected from two inpatients of the First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University and a student of a university. These samples were pretreated in accordance with a previously reported method [19,29] with slight modifications. Briefly, a certain volume aliquot of the urine sample was mixed with equal volume of 1.0 × 10−2 mol L−1 Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 8.6), shaken well, and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was then heated at 80 °C for 10 min, cooled to room temperature, and centrifuged under the same conditions. Next, the second supernatant was mixed well with anhydrous ethanol (1:3, v/v) in a refrigerator (−20 °C) overnight. After the overnight samples were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C, the precipitates were dissolved in 0.01 mol L−1 Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 8.6) and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C. The final residue was obtained, and the ethanol was removed. Subsequently, the mixture was dissolved in a certain volume of deionized water and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant obtained was used for analysis and measurement.

Nitrogen-doped CQDs with good water solubility and high stability were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using 4-aminosalicylic acid and glutamic acid as carbon and nitrogen sources. The distribution and morphology of the synthesized CQDs were characterized through TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the CQDs are spherical and well dispersed without any agglomeration. The spherical particle size is mainly concentrated at 1–3.5 nm, and the average particle size is 1.93 ± 0.46 nm, as shown in Fig. 1B. Fig. 1C shows an XRD pattern of the prepared CQDs, which exhibits a broad diffraction peak around 2θ = 23°, indicating the amorphous carbon structure inside the CQDs. The interlayer spacing of CQDs (d = 0.38 nm) is larger than that of graphite carbon (d002 = 0.34 nm), and the degree of graphitization is reduced. This finding can be ascribed to the addition of more oxygen-containing groups in CQDs [30].

The surface functional groups of solid CQDs powder were first characterized by the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1D). The broad absorption band in the region of 3100–3350 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, and the absorption band at 2609 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H. The peak at approximately 1615 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of CC in the aromatic skeleton or CO bending vibration. The absorption band at 1458 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of CH2 [31]. The peak at 1250 cm−1 indicates the presence of C–O bonds, and the absorption in the 680–965 cm−1 range is the out-of-plane bending vibration of C–H aromatic bonds. FTIR spectra showed that the surface of CQDs contained water-soluble groups, such as O–H, COOH, and –NH2 groups, indicating that the synthesized CQDs had good water solubility.

The composition and surface element state of CQDs were further studied via XPS (Fig. 2 ). The XPS spectra of CQDs had three distinct characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV (C1s), 399.8 eV (N1s), and 532.1 eV (O1s), indicating that the CQDs contain three elements, that is, C, N, and O (Fig. 2A). In addition, XPS demonstrated the elemental composition of CQDs, which had atomic contents of 70.87%, 7.09%, and 22.05%. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2B) showed four peaks: CC/C–C (284.6 eV), C–N (285.7 eV), CO (288.4 eV), and C–OH (286.4 eV). The high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2C) showed two peaks at 399.6 and 400.2 eV, which were attributed to C–N–C and N–H, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2D) showed two peaks at 531.5 and 532.6 eV, which could be attributed to CO and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively. The XPS results were basically consistent with the FTIR results. These data demonstrated that the surface of CQDs contained functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, endowing them with excellent water solubility without further chemical modification.

The optical properties of the prepared CQDs were measured by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3A shows the fluorescence spectra of the prepared CQDs at excitation wavelength range of 350–420 nm. The fluorescence emission peak position does not show a significant blue or red shift trend as the excitation wavelength increases and exhibited an excitation-independent optical property. The reason may be that the surface of CQDs has a single chromophore structure [32]. The CQDs showed the strongest fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. Fig. 3B showed that CQDs have a strong absorption peak at 275 nm, corresponding to the π–π∗ transition of the CC bond, and the absorption peak at 386 nm was attributed to the n–π∗ transition of the CO bond. The largest fluorescence emission peak was observed at 522 nm when the excitation wavelength was 390 nm. The diluted solutions of CQDs were nearly colorless (or a very light yellow color) under visible light and displayed a strong green fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3B, inset). The quantum yield of CQDs was 12.9% (quinine sulfate as a standard), indicating good fluorescence performance.

In general, the occurrence of FRET process is mainly related to two factors: the degree of spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, and the mutual distance between the donor and the acceptor (<10 nm). Fig. 4 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the citrate-stabilized AuNPs colloid. As shown in the figure, a strong characteristic surface plasmon resonance absorption peak was located at around 520 nm (curve a). In a previous study [33], the molar extinction coefficient of AuNPs (maximum absorption at 520 nm) was 2.7 × 108 M−1 cm−1. Hence, according to the Lambert–Beer law, the molar concentration of AuNPs was calculated to be 6.96 nM. CQDs showed a maximum fluorescence emission at 522 nm (curve b). The CQDs emission spectrum and the AuNPs absorption spectrum overlapped in a large part. The zeta potentials of CQDs and AuNPs (Fig. S1) were +40.1 and −19.3 mV, respectively, which facilitates the bonding between CQDs and AuNPs through electrostatic interaction. Therefore, an efficient FRET process might have occurred between them.

In the presence of different AuNPs concentration, the fluorescence spectra of CQDs at 522 nm were recorded to further confirm the above conclusion. Fig. 5 A shows the fluorescence spectra of the CQD–AuNP mixed system at different AuNPs concentration after excitation at 390 nm. The fluorescence of CQDs gradually quenched with the increase of AuNPs concentration. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (Eq) can reach 84.2% when the AuNPs concentration was 2.088 nmol L−1. The fluorescence quenching constant (KSV) was calculated according to the Stern–Volmer equation:
(1)
F 0
/ F
=
1
+
K SV
[ AuNPs ]
where [AuNPs] is the AuNPs concentration and F
0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the absence and presence of AuNPs, respectively. The relationship between F
0 /F and increasing AuNPs concentration is shown in Fig. 5B. The correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.997 in a concentration range of 0–1.044 nmol L−1, and KSV was calculated to be 1.77 × 109 L mol−1 (Fig. 5B, inset). Obviously, this large quenching constant indicates the good energy transfer interaction between CQDs and AuNPs.

The fluorescence and absorption spectra of CQDs, CQDs–AuNPs, and CQDs–AuNPs–MTs were recorded at the same condition (λex = 390 nm). As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was the strongest with the optimal emission peak at 522 nm (Fig. 6A, curve a), and no obvious changes were observed after the addition of MTs to the CQDs solution (Fig. 6A, curve b), suggesting no interaction between CQDs and MTs. The characteristic absorption peak of AuNPs was around 520 nm (Fig. 6B, curve b), and MTs showed few absorption signals in the range of 300–600 nm (Fig. 6B, curve c). When AuNPs were added, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was significantly quenched (Fig. 6A, curve c). AuNPs coated with citrate were negatively charged due to the large amount of citrate anion in the solution. However, the CQDs were positively charged at pH < 6.0 [34]. Thus, when the AuNPs mixed with CQDs, they formed CQD–AuNP nanocomposites through electrostatic interaction, and the absorption peak of AuNPs was red shifted to approximately 598 nm (Fig. 6B curve d). As a result, an obvious fluorescence quenching occurred via FRET. Meanwhile, the color of the solution changed from red (Fig. 6B, inset b) to blue (Fig. 6B, inset d). The addition of MTs to the AuNPs solution caused the AuNPs absorption peak to move from 520 nm to 620 nm (Fig. 6B curve e), and the color changed from red (Fig. 6B, inset b) to purple (Fig. 6B, inset e), indicating the aggregation of AuNPs due to the binding of AuNPs to MTs [19]. When MTs were added in the CQD–AuNP system, a significant enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of the system was observed (Fig. 6A, curve d), the absorption peak was red shifted to 640 nm (Fig. 6B, curve f), and the color of the mixed solution darkened, which were attributed to the sulfhydryl group of MTs having stronger ability to bind with AuNPs via the Au–S bond compared with CQDs. Therefore, the CQDs would be released from the CQD–AuNP nanocomposite, and the fluorescence of the system was restored.

The TEM shown in Fig. 7 further confirmed the mechanism of CQD–AuNP nanocomposite to detect MTs, and the size and aggregation state of the CQD–AuNP nanocomposite were characterized with and without MTs. As shown in Fig. 7A, AuNPs were roughly spherical, with an average diameter of about 13 ± 1 nm and good dispersion. Fig. 7B shows that the addition of AuNPs to the CQDs solution caused the aggregation of CQDs on the surface of AuNPs, and the particle size of the CQD–AuNP nanocomposite became larger (Fig. 7C), which confirmed that the positively charged CQDs and negatively charged and citrate-stabilized AuNPs were successfully combined by electrostatic interaction. However, Fig. 7D shows that when MTs were added, the CQDs bound by AuNPs decreased and showed strong aggregation, and CQDs were separated from the CQD–AuNP nanocomposite, indicating that MTs interacted with AuNPs. The sulfur atoms of thiol groups in MTs bind to AuNPs by forming Au–S bonds, and have a strong binding preference toward AuNPs, which promotes the aggregation of AuNPs and changes the color of the solution. Therefore, MTs can be detected by fluorescence recovery of CQDs.

On the basis of the above results, a schematic of the method for detecting MTs is shown in Scheme 1 . When CQDs were added into AuNPs, CQDs were adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs through electrostatic interactions, resulting in energy transfer between CQDs and AuNPs. The fluorescence emission of CQDs was quenched by FRET, showing an “off state.” After the addition of MTs, the MTs and AuNPs bound through Au–S bonds, which inhibited the FRET effect between CQDs and AuNPs and destroyed the structure of the CQD–AuNP nanocomposite, resulting in the release of CQDs from the system. Eventually, the fluorescence emission of CQDs is “turned on.”
The effects of some factors related to the analysis conditions were further studied as follows.

The effect of solution pH on the fluorescence intensity of the system was investigated by changing the pH values of the citrate buffer. The fluorescence intensity was highly dependent on pH values (Fig. S2). The ΔF would gradually increase with increasing pH values. The maximum ΔF was obtained at pH 3.0 and then decreased significantly in the range of 3.0–6.0. Therefore, MTs were positively charged when the solution pH was less than the isoelectric point of MTs (pI is around 4.0), and the AuNPs had a negative charge at pH ≥ 3, which facilitated the binding of MTs to AuNPs but destroyed the structure of CQD–AuNP nanocomposite. At pH < 3, the negative charge density on the AuNP surface might decrease, and the stability of AuNPs was lowered at the same time, affecting the FRET between CQDs and AuNPs. Therefore, solution with a pH of 3 was selected for further experiments.

The effect of AuNP concentration on the fluorescence recovery of the system was studied (Fig. S3). A good fluorescence recovery efficiency was observed at a relatively low MT concentration when the AuNP concentration was low; however, the linear range was narrow. When the concentration of AuNPs was high, the fluorescence recovery efficiency had a linear relationship with the MT concentration in a large range. However, the response was weak at low MT concentration. Therefore, considering the wide linear range and high sensitivity, the AuNP concentration was selected to be 2.09 nM for the whole experiment.

The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the system and the reaction time is shown in Fig. S4. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs dramatically decreased and reached equilibrium in 1 min, and 5 min was selected as the optimal reaction time to ensure stability of the whole detection system and reduce experimental errors. After the addition of MTs to the CQD–AuNP mixed solution, the fluorescence intensity of the system increased gradually as the reaction time increased and reached a steady state at 15 min. Therefore, 15 min was used as the optimal incubation time for the following experiments.

During the experiment, temperature also had an effect on the fluorescence recovery of the system within a certain period of time. As shown in Fig. S5, ΔF gradually increased with increasing temperature and reached a maximum at 50 °C. The reason may be that the CQD–AuNP nanocomposite is unstable at high temperatures, making AuNPs easier to bind to MTs, whereas MTs will be lost at high temperatures. Therefore, 50 °C was chosen as the optimal temperature for further studies.

Compared with the methods mentioned in Table 1 , the presented method possesses advantages such as low LOD and a wide linear range for MT analysis, which are better than those obtained by other methods, such as electrochemical analysis, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, and HPLC-AAS [29,36,37]. Moreover, the FRET-based method is simple and easy to construct. Furthermore, CQDs have low cytotoxicity and favorable biocompatibility compared with the assays based on organic fluorescent dyes or semiconductor QDs.

Under optimal conditions, the selectivity and recognition ability of CQD–AuNP probes for different metal cations, inorganic anions, and organic compounds were investigated. As shown in Fig. 9 , the method developed in this study has high selectivity for MTs against other small molecules containing thiol groups. However, their thiol groups also have a high affinity toward AuNPs. This may be due to MTs having stronger affinity than other thiol compounds because the 20 cysteinyl sulfurs of MTs function as bridging and terminal ligands for coordination [19]. Furthermore, the interference of cysteine can be eliminated during sample pre-processing. Therefore, cysteine and glutathione or other thiol compounds have trifling interference when detecting MTs at low concentration. In addition, the metal ions have particularly weak function on selectivity, even if the concentration of metal ions is hundreds of times higher than that of MTs. Therefore, the method developed in this study has a very good selectivity for MTs detection.

The developed method was applied to detect MTs in human urine samples under the optimized experimental conditions to assess its reliability for testing real samples. The urine samples were pretreated, and then recovery tests were performed through the addition of known amounts of MTs to the samples to evaluate the method’s accuracy. The obtained results are summarized in Table 2 . The results obtained using the developed method were comparable to those obtained from UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. UV–Vis spectroscopy experiment was operated using Ellman’s reagent quantification method modified by Viarengo et al. [38]: 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) is reacted with thiolate group in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 8.0) and forms TNB with a yellow color. The absorbance value (A) was measured at a wavelength of 412 nm because the TNB had maximum absorption at 412 nm, and the corresponding sulfydryl-concentration was calculated on the basis of the standard curve according to its absorbance [39]. Statistical analysis was performed on all of these results using Student’s t-test. Student’s t-values with a confidence level of 95% did not exceed the theoretical values (Table 2), indicating that there were no significant differences between these two methods. Thus, the proposed FRET-based detection method for MTs was found to be reliable, sensitive, and practical.

We constructed a CQD–AuNP sensing system based on FRET between CQDs and AuNPs, where CQDs act as the donor and the citrate-stabilized AuNPs as the acceptor, which can rapidly, sensitively, and selectively detect MTs. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs by AuNPs can be effectively restored by the addition of MTs. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence recovery efficiency of the system was proportional to the MTs concentration over a wide range of 12 nM–210 nM, with an LOD of 5.25 nM. This FRET-based assay displayed many advantages, such as high sensitivity and selectivity and easy operation. Moreover, considering the low cost and absence of cytotoxicity of this method, we expect that this method will be further applied for the rapid and convenient detection of MTs in vivo.

Lian Duan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xiaoyu Du: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Huijun Zhao: Investigation. Yue Sun: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wen Liu: Resources, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (21301126); Shanxi Province Youth Science and Technology Research Fund (2013021009-3, 201701D221038); Shanxi University Science and Technology Innovation Project (173030107-S).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.05.054.
